title: SQL Subqueries & CTEs
description: Subqueries, correlated subqueries, CTEs, recursive queries, and set operations.

questions.text: What is a scalar subquery?

options.text: A subquery that returns multiple rows and columns
A subquery that returns exactly one row and one column (a single value)
A subquery in the FROM clause
A subquery that uses aggregate functions only

questions.text: Which query uses a scalar subquery to find employees who earn more than the company average?

options.text: SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE Salary > AVG(Salary)
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE Salary > (SELECT AVG(Salary) FROM Employees)
SELECT * FROM Employees HAVING Salary > AVG(Salary)
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE Salary > AVERAGE

questions.text: What is a correlated subquery?

options.text: A subquery that is executed once and its result is reused for all outer rows
A subquery that references a column from the outer query and is re-evaluated for each outer row
A subquery with multiple SELECT statements
A subquery that returns multiple columns

questions.text: What does EXISTS check in SQL?

options.text: Whether a column contains any non-NULL values
Whether a subquery returns at least one row
Whether a table exists in the database
Whether all rows in the subquery match a condition

questions.text: Which query finds customers who have placed at least one order using EXISTS?

options.text: SELECT * FROM Customers c WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Orders o WHERE o.CustomerID = c.ID)
SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE EXISTS Orders
SELECT * FROM Customers c WHERE c.ID EXISTS IN Orders
SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE EXISTS (SELECT * FROM Orders)

questions.text: What does NOT EXISTS return?

options.text: TRUE when the subquery returns one or more rows
TRUE when the subquery returns no rows
It always returns NULL
The same as NOT IN in all cases

questions.text: How does IN with a subquery work? Example: WHERE ID IN (SELECT CustomerID FROM Orders)

options.text: Returns rows where ID matches any value returned by the subquery
Returns rows where ID matches all values returned by the subquery
Returns rows where ID does not match any subquery value
This syntax is not valid

questions.text: What is a derived table (inline view)?

options.text: A permanent table created from a SELECT statement
A subquery used in the FROM clause that acts as a temporary named result set
A table that is created from a view
A table with computed columns

questions.text: Which is a valid example of a derived table?

options.text: SELECT * FROM (SELECT ID, Name FROM Employees WHERE Active = 1) AS ActiveEmp
SELECT * DERIVED FROM Employees
FROM (SELECT ID, Name FROM Employees) JOIN Orders
CREATE DERIVED TABLE ActiveEmp AS SELECT ID FROM Employees

questions.text: What does the WITH keyword introduce in SQL?

options.text: A transaction block
A Common Table Expression (CTE)
A stored procedure
A database connection setting

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct syntax for a CTE?

options.text: CTE MyCTE AS (SELECT ID, Name FROM Employees)
WITH MyCTE AS (SELECT ID, Name FROM Employees) SELECT * FROM MyCTE
CREATE CTE MyCTE AS SELECT ID, Name FROM Employees
SELECT * INTO CTE MyCTE FROM Employees

questions.text: What is the main advantage of a CTE over a subquery?

options.text: CTEs are always faster than subqueries
CTEs improve readability and can be referenced multiple times in the same query
CTEs persist after the query completes
CTEs allow the use of aggregate functions

questions.text: What makes a CTE recursive?

options.text: It references itself using UNION ALL within the CTE definition
It calls an external stored procedure
It uses a loop inside the WITH block
It is nested inside another CTE

questions.text: A recursive CTE has two parts — what are they called?

options.text: Base case and loop body
Anchor member and recursive member
Root query and branch query
Initial select and repeat select

questions.text: Can you define multiple CTEs in a single WITH clause?

options.text: No, only one CTE is allowed per query
Yes — separate multiple CTEs with commas inside a single WITH clause
Yes, but you need a separate WITH keyword for each
Only if they are not related to each other

questions.text: In which clause must the final query that uses a CTE appear?

options.text: Inside the WITH block itself
Immediately after the WITH...AS(...) definition
In a separate batch
Inside a stored procedure only

questions.text: What does EXCEPT return?

options.text: Rows that exist in both queries
Rows from the first query that are NOT present in the second query
Rows from the second query that are NOT present in the first query
All rows from both queries combined

questions.text: What does INTERSECT return?

options.text: All rows from both queries including duplicates
Rows that appear in both result sets
Rows that appear in only one of the result sets
An error if the two queries have different columns

questions.text: Which set operation is equivalent to a JOIN-based approach of finding rows in one table but not another?

options.text: UNION
INTERSECT
EXCEPT (or MINUS in Oracle)
CROSS JOIN

questions.text: What is the key performance difference between a correlated subquery and a non-correlated subquery?

options.text: They always have identical performance
A correlated subquery is re-executed for each outer row; a non-correlated subquery executes once
Non-correlated subqueries are always slower
Correlated subqueries cannot use indexes

questions.text: What is the difference between EXISTS and IN when using a subquery?

options.text: EXISTS checks for row existence and stops at first match; IN compares against a complete list of values
IN is faster than EXISTS in all cases
EXISTS returns a value; IN returns a boolean
There is no difference

questions.text: Can a CTE be used with INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statements?

options.text: No, CTEs can only be used with SELECT
Yes — CTEs can precede and be referenced by DML statements
Only with INSERT, not UPDATE or DELETE
Only in Oracle, not SQL Server

questions.text: Which query finds the second highest salary using a subquery?

options.text: SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employees WHERE Salary < (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employees)
SELECT SECOND MAX(Salary) FROM Employees
SELECT TOP 2 Salary FROM Employees ORDER BY Salary DESC
SELECT MAX(Salary) - 1 FROM Employees

questions.text: What is a common use case for recursive CTEs?

options.text: Performing aggregate calculations
Traversing hierarchical data such as org charts or bill of materials
Generating random numbers
Replacing GROUP BY operations

questions.text: What happens if a subquery used with IN returns a NULL value?

options.text: NULL is ignored in the comparison
Rows that should match may not be returned as expected due to NULL comparison logic
An error is raised
NULL is treated as 0

questions.text: What requirement must both queries meet when using UNION, INTERSECT, or EXCEPT?

options.text: They must reference the same table
They must return the same number of columns with compatible data types
They must have the same WHERE conditions
They must both use GROUP BY

questions.text: In a chained CTE (WITH cte1 AS (...), cte2 AS (...)), can cte2 reference cte1?

options.text: No, each CTE is independent
Yes — a later CTE in the WITH clause can reference an earlier one
Only if they are in separate WITH clauses
Only if cte1 is recursive

questions.text: What does the query: SELECT * FROM A WHERE ID IN (SELECT ID FROM B) — return?

options.text: All rows from A where the ID also exists in B
All rows from B where the ID also exists in A
All rows from both A and B
Rows from A that are NOT in B

questions.text: How is a subquery in the SELECT list (scalar subquery) different from one in the WHERE clause?

options.text: A SELECT-list subquery computes a value per row for display; a WHERE-clause subquery filters rows
SELECT-list subqueries can return multiple rows
WHERE-clause subqueries return a single value for display
There is no difference in behavior

questions.text: Which keyword prevents a recursive CTE from running infinitely in SQL Server?

options.text: LIMIT RECURSION
STOP RECURSION
OPTION (MAXRECURSION n) — defaults to 100 in SQL Server
RECURSIVE LIMIT n
